versions.label: BSB
paragraphs: 17 After Abram returned from defeating Chedorlaomer and the kings allied with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet him in the Valley of Shaveh (that is, the King’s Valley).
18 Then Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine—since he was priest of God Most High — 19 and he blessed Abram and said:
“Blessed be Abram by God Most High,
20 and blessed be God Most High,
who has delivered your enemies into your hand.”
Then Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
21 The king of Sodom said to Abram, “Give me the people, but take the goods for yourself.”

22 But Abram replied to the king of Sodom, “I have raised my hand to the LORD God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth, 23 that I will not accept even a thread, or a strap of a sandal, or anything that belongs to you, lest you should say, ‘I have made Abram rich.’ 24 I will accept nothing but what my men have eaten and the share for the men who went with me—Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. They may take their portion.”

context_lines: In **Genesis 14**, this section should be read within the flow of the chapter and the larger movement of Genesis.
common_interpretation_lines: A common reading of **Melchizedek Blesses Abram** is that it should be interpreted in light of the chapter's full narrative movement rather than as an isolated proof text.
Where interpreters differ on details, the immediate setting in **Genesis 14** remains the first control on meaning.
current_position_lines: This site's current position is that Melchizedek matters here not merely as an interesting Old Testament figure, but as the textual groundwork for the priesthood argument later developed in Hebrews. He appears as both king and priest, without the genealogical framework that defines the Levitical order.

That is why this passage becomes important in the study of church leadership and priesthood. The New Testament's appeal to Melchizedek is part of the case that priestly mediation is centered in Christ rather than passed down through a clerical class.
